skill.name: github-code-review
skill.description: Coordinate AI-assisted GitHub pull request reviews with swarm-style agents, GitHub CLI workflows, and quality gates.
skill.summary: Run structured AI code reviews for GitHub pull requests.
skill.author: Claude Code Flow

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found extensive external command usage, GitHub token access, and network links. Most matches are documentation examples for a GitHub review skill, but the webhook handler example uses unsanitized GitHub event and comment data inside shell commands, creating a real command injection risk if copied. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found, so the skill is not blocked, but it is not safe to publish without remediation.

high_findings.title: Command Injection in Webhook Handler Example
high_findings.description: The JavaScript webhook sample imports child_process.execSync and interpolates pull request and comment fields into shell command strings. A GitHub comment beginning with /swarm is user controlled, so copying this pattern could execute unintended shell content on the webhook host.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The finding combines direct child_process use with template literals that include webhook event data. The comment command value is derived from GitHub user input and is passed to execSync without argument separation or escaping.

medium_findings.title: Extensive External Command Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill repeatedly instructs users to run gh, npx ruv-swarm, jq, grep, sed, and shell command substitutions. This is expected for a GitHub automation skill, but it increases operational risk because commands act on repository data and PR content.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The external command patterns are real and numerous, but most are task-relevant examples rather than hidden behavior. Risk depends on how safely users adapt the commands.

medium_findings.title: GitHub Token Access in CI Example
medium_findings.description: The GitHub Actions example pipes secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN into gh auth login. This is a common workflow pattern, but it requires least-privilege token permissions and careful handling because the workflow later posts reviews, labels, and comments.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token usage is explicit and legitimate for GitHub CLI automation. The sample does not show token exfiltration, but it does not document permission minimization.

low_findings.title: Network Links Are Documentation References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to GitHub CLI, ruv-swarm, and Claude Flow documentation. They do not show data exfiltration or runtime network calls inside the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are plain markdown links in an Additional Resources section. No evidence found that these URLs receive credentials or repository data.

low_findings.title: Sensitive File, Weak Crypto, and Reconnaissance Matches Dismissed
low_findings.description: The Windows SAM matches are caused by SECURITY_RESULTS text, and weak cryptography matches are review terminology such as description, design, SOLID, DRY, and task placeholders. The system reconnaissance matches are checklist or optimization text, not commands that inspect the host.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line context shows documentation and placeholder text rather than access to Windows SAM, cryptographic APIs, or host reconnaissance commands. No evidence found of those specific behaviors.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Execution with Template Literal Interpolation
dangerous_patterns.description: The webhook sample passes template literals to execSync while embedding GitHub event fields. This pattern should be replaced with argument arrays and strict command validation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code shows direct shell execution with interpolated values. The event comment body is user influenced and appears inside a shell command string.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Substitution over Pull Request Data
dangerous_patterns.description: Several examples capture PR data, diffs, and generated comments through shell command substitution. This is functional for automation, but implementers should treat PR content as untrusted input.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command substitutions are visible and process PR data. They are not automatically malicious, but they create a meaningful trust-boundary concern.

content.user_title: Review GitHub Pull Requests with AI Agents
content.value_statement: Manual pull request review can miss security, performance, and architecture issues. This skill provides structured GitHub review workflows that coordinate specialized AI agents and quality gates.
actual_capabilities: Defines workflows for reviewing GitHub pull requests with specialized review agents.
Shows GitHub CLI commands for collecting PR files, metadata, diffs, labels, and review state.
Provides examples for security, performance, architecture, style, and accessibility review focus areas.
Includes CI workflow patterns for automated PR review comments, labels, and review decisions.
Describes quality gates, custom agent configuration, and review report formats.
limitations: Requires GitHub CLI, ruv-swarm, and claude-flow tooling to be installed and authenticated.
Examples need security hardening before use in webhook or CI environments.
Review quality depends on repository context, model behavior, and configured review criteria.
Does not replace required human approval for high-risk production changes.

use_cases.title: Review Security-Sensitive Pull Requests
use_cases.description: Run a focused review for OWASP risks, secret exposure, dependency concerns, and authorization flaws before merge.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team PR Feedback
use_cases.description: Create repeatable review criteria across style, architecture, performance, accessibility, and test coverage.

use_cases.title: Automate CI Review Summaries
use_cases.description: Use GitHub Actions and GitHub CLI examples to post structured review summaries, labels, and quality gate decisions.

prompt_templates.title: Basic PR Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of one pull request
prompt_templates.prompt: Review pull request 123 for correctness, readability, and test coverage. Summarize the most important issues first.

prompt_templates.title: Security-Focused Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review pull request 123 for authentication, authorization, secret handling, dependency, and input validation risks. Include severity and suggested fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Domain Review Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a review plan for pull request 123 with security, performance, architecture, accessibility, and style agents. Define expected outputs for each agent.

prompt_templates.title: Quality Gate Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced CI and merge readiness review
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a GitHub review workflow for pull request 123 that gathers PR context, runs specialized reviews, posts findings, applies labels, and blocks critical issues.

output_examples.input: Review a pull request that changes login and session handling.
output_examples.output: The review highlights authentication bypass risk, missing session expiration tests, and a recommended change to centralize authorization checks.

output_examples.input: Generate a review summary for a large frontend pull request.
output_examples.output: The summary separates accessibility issues, rendering performance concerns, style comments, and merge-blocking defects.

output_examples.input: Assess whether a pull request is ready for auto-merge.
output_examples.output: The result states that merge should wait because security review passed, but performance tests and two required approvals are missing.

best_practices: Run commands with least-privilege GitHub tokens and repository permissions.
Treat pull request titles, bodies, diffs, comments, and labels as untrusted input.
Keep automated review comments concise, actionable, and linked to specific changed files.
anti_patterns: Do not pass GitHub comment text or PR metadata directly into shell commands.
Do not let automated review approval replace required human review for sensitive changes.
Do not post noisy, duplicate, or low-confidence findings on every pull request.

faq.question: What tools does this skill expect?
faq.answer: It expects GitHub CLI, ruv-swarm, and claude-flow in the local or CI environment.

faq.question: Can it review security issues?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes security review workflows for OWASP risks, secrets, permissions, and dependency concerns.

faq.question: Can it post comments to GitHub?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples show GitHub CLI and API patterns for review comments, labels, and review decisions.

faq.question: Is it safe to copy the webhook example directly?
faq.answer: No. The webhook example needs command injection hardening before production use.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: Yes. GitHub CLI operations and referenced tools need network access to GitHub and package sources.

faq.question: Does it replace a human reviewer?
faq.answer: No. It helps organize and accelerate review, but humans should approve important production changes.
